summary: Gruber reflects on the anonymous Republican official's November 2020 quote asking 'What's the downside for humoring him?' about Trump's election fraud claims, calling it the quote of the year. He argues the quote aged catastrophically given the January 6th Capitol riot. He extends the critique beyond the GOP to Facebook and Twitter, whose leadership similarly dismissed the real-world consequences of tolerating election misinformation. The core argument is that society is dangerously bad at imagining norm-shattering consequences, too easily conflating things that haven't happened with things that can't happen.
key_points: The anonymous GOP official's dismissive quote about humoring Trump's fraud claims became a defining failure of imagination when the Capitol riot followed
We collectively conflate things that merely haven't happened with things that 'can't' happen, a dangerous cognitive failure
If people truly believe an election was stolen, riotous dissent becomes a logical response — the violence was foreseeable
The same 'what's the downside?' negligence applies to Facebook and Twitter, who ignored their own data scientists' warnings about misinformation fueling violence
Some ideas — like a Big Lie about a fair election — should never be humored, just as bomb threats and 911 prank calls aren't
money_quotes: We easily conflate things that merely *haven't* happened with things that "can't" happen.
The downside for "humoring him for this little bit of time" should have been obvious to all: a historic, violent, calamity.
If you truly believe that an election was stolen, riotous dissent is a reasonable course of action.
"What's the downside?" *is* the right question. The problem is the way the Republican Party, and to a lesser extent, Facebook and Twitter, approached the answer.
There are some ideas that should never be humored.
This stuff is loony, but it keeps them engaged. People post all sorts of crazy stuff, but they're not really going to riot, right?
key_insight: The catastrophic failure behind January 6th was not a failure of information but a failure of imagination — the refusal to believe that tolerating lies about democracy could produce real-world violence.
